title: Soft Targets and Logit Matching
concept: The student is trained to match the raw output probabilities (logits) of the teacher, capturing the teacher's uncertainty and nuance.

exercises.prompt: What are 'logits' in a neural network?

options.text: Raw, unscaled output scores prior to probability conversion.
The final layer that only outputs 0 or 1.
A method for deleting bad data from the dataset.

exercises.explain: Logits are the raw, pre-activation values that contain the rich, soft distribution of the model's knowledge before it is simplified.

cards.title: Beyond Right and Wrong
cards.body: When a teacher model helps a smaller student model learn, does it just hand over the final correct answers? No! It shares its 'soft targets'—the raw scores that show exactly how unsure or confident it was about every single option.

cards.title: What is Logit Matching?
cards.body: Logits are the raw, unpolished scores a model calculates before turning them into final percentages. Logit matching is a training method where we force the student model to copy these raw scores directly, capturing all the subtle hints and secondary guesses the teacher had.

cards.title: The Master Chef's Recipe
cards.body: Think of a master chef teaching an apprentice. Instead of just telling them 'this dish is a 10/10' (hard label), the chef says: 'This is 80% sweet, 15% sour, and 5% salty.' By matching these exact proportions (logits), the apprentice learns the delicate balance of flavors, not just a pass/fail grade.

cards.title: Seeing the Nuance
cards.body: When looking at a blurry image of a cat, a teacher model might output logits that translate to: 70% Cat, 28% Dog, and 2% Car. Instead of just learning 'Cat', the student model learns that this image also looks a lot like a dog, capturing the teacher's realistic uncertainty.

cards.title: Is Uncertainty Bad?
cards.body: Many believe that a student should only learn from 100% correct, sharp answers. But in AI, learning from the teacher's hesitation and near-misses (like mistaking a wolf for a dog) actually makes the student much smarter and more flexible!

cards.title: Hard Labels vs. Soft Logits
cards.body: Compare how a student learns using hard targets (final answers only) versus soft targets (logits).

left.text: Output: 100% Cat. The student learns nothing about how similar the image is to a dog or a tiger.

right.text: Output: 75% Cat, 20% Tiger, 5% Mug. The student learns the relationships and visual similarities between classes.

exercises.prompt: What characterizes 'soft targets' in model training?

options.text: They only show the single best answer.
They show probabilities spread across multiple possible answers.
They ignore all wrong answers completely.
They are always whole numbers like 0 or 1.

exercises.explain: Soft targets are probabilities that distribute confidence across multiple classes (e.g., 70% cat, 25% dog), whereas hard targets are binary (e.g., 100% cat).

exercises.prompt: True or False: Raw logits contain more detailed information about what the model has learned than just the final, single-choice prediction.
exercises.explain: Logits contain rich, hidden relationships between classes (like how similar a cheetah is to a leopard), which is lost if we only look at the final prediction.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term to complete the sentence about neural network outputs.
exercises.sentence: To capture a teacher's exact confidence levels, the student model matches the raw values known as [blank].
exercises.explain: Logits are the raw, unnormalized outputs of a neural network before they are scaled into standard probabilities.

exercises.prompt: Which of these AI training agents is performing Logit Matching?

options.text: Model A: 'I only care if the teacher said Cat or Dog. Give me the final answer.'
Model B: 'I want the raw, pre-softmax scores so I can copy the teacher's exact balance.'
Model C: 'I will ignore the teacher and train on my own raw guesses.'

exercises.explain: Model B is using the raw logits directly to match the teacher's complete reasoning style, whereas Model A is just copying final labels and Model C is guessing randomly.

exercises.prompt: Why is logit matching considered a superior way to transfer knowledge compared to only training a student on a teacher's final, single-answer predictions?

options.text: It forces the student to make only 100% confident predictions.
It discards all information about secondary classes to save memory.
It transfers the teacher's full confidence distribution, including near-misses.
It runs the student model twice as fast during normal deployment.

exercises.explain: Logit matching forces the student to learn the precise, raw outputs (logits) of the teacher. This transfers the teacher's complete reasoning—including how close it was to making other choices—rather than just its final, simplified answers.
